findings.description: Rule is scoped only to the single campaign headline 'YOU DESERVE MORE' but carries no scope/campaign facet distinguishing it from general headline rules, and drops the explicit cross-reference '(see 1.6)' which points to governing detail elsewhere in the bible. Without a scope facet this rule risks being applied to all headlines instead of just this campaign asset.

Unit conflates two distinct facets: casing (ALL CAPS) and typeface/channel-conditional font requirement (Agenda for print, Nunito Sans Bold+ for email fallback). Clustering both under a single typography casing rule loses the channel-conditional font requirement, which should be split into its own font/typeface rule or added as an explicit facet.

Source unit is explicitly tagged [GENERAL], signaling a governance-level marker (baseline/global applicability) that is not captured as a facet on the rule. Without this tag, downstream systems cannot distinguish this as the overriding general prohibition versus a peer-level rule.

The general prohibition rule explicitly references 'the cases above' (campaign headline ALL CAPS and chart headline ALL CAPS) as approved exceptions, but no exception/override relation is recorded between this rule and rule_lisraya_campaign_headline_all_caps / rule_lisraya_chart_headline_all_caps. This missing relation could cause the must_not rule to be misapplied against the legitimate ALL CAPS exceptions.

Rule hardness is set to strong_default without capturing that it is itself overridden by two hard_constraint exceptions (campaign headline, chart headline). The relation of 'default unless exception applies' is implicit but not encoded, risking ambiguous precedence resolution.
